segments.text: Chapter 13-14 Part 3D: Enzyme Inhibition Dr. Shahir Rizk

The Aspartic Proteases
Pepsin, chymosin , cathepsin D, renin and 	HIV-1 protease
All involve two Asp residues at the active site
These two Asp residues work together as general acid-base catalysts
Aspartic proteases play many roles in humans

The Aspartic Proteases
Figure 14.22 Structures of (a) HIV-1 protease, a dimer, and (b) pepsin, a monomer.  Pepsin ’ s N-terminal half is shown in red;  the C-terminal half is shown in blue.
Most aspartic proteases exhibit a two-lobed structure.  Each lobe contributes one catalytic aspartate to the active site.  HIV-1 protease is a homodimeric enzyme, with each subunit contributing a catalytic Asp residue.

Aspartic Protease Mechanism
The key to Aspartic proteases is a set of 2 Asp residues in the active site
Aspartic proteases show one relatively low p K a , and one relatively high p K a
Aspartic proteases employ low-barrier hydrogen bonds (LBHBs) in their mechanism
The predominant catalytic factor in aspartic proteases is general acid-base catalysis

Low-Barrier Hydrogen Bonds (LBHBs)
Figure 14.13  Energy diagrams for conventional H bonds (a), and low-barrier hydrogen bonds ( b) .

A Mechanism for the Aspartic Proteases
Mechanism for the aspartic proteases. LBHBs play a role in the mechanism

HIV-1 Protease: drug target
HIV -1 protease cleaves the polyprotein to produce active viral proteins
This makes it an attractive drug target
Inhibition of HIV-1 protease  less active virus, slow down viral proliferation
How can we design a drug to inhibit the function of HIV-1 protease based on our knowledge of how enzymes work?

HIV-1 Protease: drug target
Enzymes have a high affinity for TS
A stable molecule that mimics the structure of the TS, but is chemically inert would do the following:
Bind with very high affinity to the active site
Compete with the natural substrate
Prevent reaction from taking place
This type of molecule is known as a Transition state analog

HIV-1 protease inhibitors
Since Asp proteases go through a tetrahedral intermediate, all HIV protease inhibitors have an essential sp 3 carbon with an –OH resembling the transition state
Show Protein structure

Protease Inhibitors: TS analogs (tetrahedral structure)
Protease inhibitor drugs used by AIDS patients

HIV-1 protease inhibitors
Inhibitor occupies the active site
Competes with  substrate binding
Prevent catalysis
Substrate peptide: yellow
Inhibitor ( Viracept ): cyan
Catalytic aspartates: red

Drug design: Enzyme inhibitors
Enzyme inhibitors are a major class of drugs
Naturally occurring molecules regulate enzyme activity in response to physiological conditions
So, what are the current model(s) for enzyme inhibition?

13.4 What Can Be Learned from the Inhibition of Enzyme Activity?
Enzymes may be inhibited reversibly or irreversibly
Reversible inhibitors may bind at the active site or at some other site
Enzymes may also be inhibited in an irreversible manner
Penicillin is an irreversible suicide inhibitor

Types of enzyme inhibition
Irreversible inhibition
Typically involves an inhibitor molecule binding covalently (irreversibly) to the enzyme
Usually binding to a catalytic side chain, preventing catalysis
Result: “permanent” deactivation of the enzyme
Often called “suicide” inhibitor

Suicide inhibitor: Penicillin
Penicillin is an antibiotic, inhibits the enzyme transpeptidase
Transpeptidase : biosynthesis of bacterial cell-wall
Penicillin forms a covalent-enzyme complex that renders the enzyme inactive, blocking cell-wall synthesis and inhibiting bacterial growth

Types of reversible inhibition
Competitive inhibition:
Inhibitor binds to the active site
Higher Km, same Vmax
Non-competitive inhibition:
Inhibitor binds away from active site
Same Km, lower Vmax
Uncompetitive inhibition:
Inhibitor binds to ES complex
Lower Km, lower Vmax

Competitive inhibition
E can bind either S or I, but not both
Apparent affinity of E to S goes down
Km increases (need high [S] to reach ½ Vmax )
Vmax is unchanged
Competitive Inhibitors typically resembles the structure of S
No product is formed
Many comp. Inhibitors are TS analogs
Example: HIV protease inhibitors

Competitive inhibitor: graphs
Rate vs [S] plot
A good way to visualize inhibition is through a Lineweaver -Burk plot (double reciprocal plot)

Lineweaver–Burk plot
Double reciprocal plot
Plot: 1/rate vs. 1/[S] (instead of rate vs. [S])
Rate
[Substrate]
Vmax

1/[Substrate]
-1/Km is the X intercept and 1/Vmax is the Y intercept

Linear Plots Can Be Derived from the Michaelis-Menten Equation
Do not need to derive this equation
Lineweaver -Burk:
Begin with v = V max [S]/( K m + [S]) and take the reciprocal of both sides
Rearrange to obtain the Lineweaver -Burk equation:
A plot of 1/ v versus 1/[S] should yield a straight line
Like: y = mx + b

Linear Plots Can Be Derived from the Michaelis-Menten Equation
Figure 13.9 The Lineweaver-Burk double-reciprocal plot.
Mathematical trick to convert a hyperbolic curve into a straight line
Much easier to fit a straight line

Competitive Inhibitors Compete With Substrate for the Same Site on the Enzyme
Figure 13.13 Lineweaver -Burk plot of competitive inhibition, showing lines for no I, and increasing concentrations of I
Competitive changes the Km (increased Km)
No effect on Vmax

Non-competitive inhibition
Inhibitor binds far away from the active site
Can bind to E or ES
Result:
typically altering the protein conformation
Slower turnover rate (lower Vmax )
No effect on Km
Reduced rate
Normal rate

Pure Noncompetitive Inhibition – where S and I bind to different sites on the enzyme
Lineweaver -Burk plot of pure noncompetitive inhibition.  Note that I does not alter K m but that it decreases V max .
Reduced Vmax
No change in Km

Uncompetitive inhibition
Inhibitor binds ONLY to ES complex, forming “ESI”
Does not bind to E in the absence of S
This increases affinity of E for S, but slows the rate
Result: lower Km AND lower Vmax
 Slow rate
 Normal rate

Uncompetitive Inhibition
Curve is shifted to the left (lower Km) and down (lower Vmax )
Lineweaver -Burk plot shows parallel lines as [I] increases
Let’s review!
